title: A Name Is Given
caption: John McCarthy, who coined the term "artificial intelligence" and co-organised the 1956 Dartmouth workshop, photographed in 2006.

overview.text: In the summer of 1956, a small workshop at Dartmouth College gathered researchers around a bold proposal: that "every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it." The meeting coined the name "artificial intelligence" and launched it as a field.

deep.text: The Dartmouth proposal set an agenda more than it delivered results: automata theory, neural nets, abstraction, creativity and language all appear on its wish-list. Its optimism — that major progress might come from ten people in a single summer — became a lasting cautionary tale about how easily the difficulty of intelligence is underestimated.

challenge.prompt: When was the term "artificial intelligence" coined?

feedback.correct: Spot on — the 1956 Dartmouth workshop gave the field its name.
feedback.incorrect: It was 1956, at the Dartmouth Summer Research Project.

sources.title: A Proposal for the Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence
sources.author: J. McCarthy, M. Minsky, N. Rochester & C. Shannon
